extra.message: OS Command Injection - user input directly concatenated into shell command
SQL Injection in authentication query due to direct string concatenation of user input
Path Traversal in content handler - user-controlled filename allows directory traversal
Path Traversal in static file handler - URL regex capture allows directory traversal
Plaintext password storage in database without hashing or encryption
Hardcoded credentials in source code for eight user accounts
Missing session management - no session creation after successful authentication
No authentication required for sensitive endpoints - all handlers lack authentication checks
Reflected XSS vulnerability via template engine raw directive bypass in search functionality
Reflected XSS in HTML context via raw template directive
Sensitive Data Logging - HTTP request body containing credentials logged to stdout in plaintext

Missing Authentication on Server/Ping Handler (POST)

The ServerHandler POST method allows any unauthenticated user to execute OS commands via the ping functionality. There is no authentication check.

Impact: Any unauthenticated user can execute arbitrary OS commands on the server through command injection in the ping functionality.

Fix: Add authentication checks to the ServerHandler to ensure only authorized users can use the ping functionality.

Hardcoded Plaintext Credentials in Database Seed

Multiple user accounts are created with plaintext passwords hardcoded directly in the source code. Passwords are stored in the SQLite database without any hashing.

Impact: Anyone with access to the source code or database file can obtain all user credentials. The admin account has a trivially guessable password ('admin').

Fix: Hash passwords using a strong algorithm like bcrypt or argon2 before storing them. Remove hardcoded credentials and use environment variables or secure configuration management.

Plaintext password storage in SQLite database

The Users table stores passwords in plaintext (Password TEXT column). The create_db function inserts raw plaintext passwords, and the login handler queries them directly without any hashing.

Impact: If the database is compromised, all user passwords are immediately exposed in cleartext. This affects PII data and violates security best practices.

Fix: Hash passwords using a strong algorithm like bcrypt or argon2 before storing them. Compare hashes during authentication instead of plaintext values.

CSRF Protection Disabled (No xsrf_cookies Setting)

The Tornado application settings do not include "xsrf_cookies": True. Tornado's built-in CSRF protection is disabled by default. All POST handlers (login, server ping, file upload) are vulnerable to cross-site request forgery attacks.

Impact: An attacker can craft malicious pages that cause authenticated users to perform unintended actions like submitting login credentials, executing server commands, or uploading files.

Fix: Add "xsrf_cookies": True to the application settings and include {% module xsrf_form_html() %} in all forms.

Unrestricted File Upload

The UploadHandler accepts file uploads without any validation of file type, content, or size. Any file type (including executable scripts, web shells, etc.) can be uploaded. The original file extension is preserved.

Impact: An attacker can upload malicious files such as web shells, executables, or files with dangerous extensions. While files are saved to /tmp/, the filename is disclosed in the response, potentially enabling further attacks.

Fix: Validate file types against an allowlist of permitted extensions and MIME types. Limit file size. Do not preserve the original extension. Scan uploaded files for malicious content.

Missing Authentication on Login Handler (POST)

The UsersHandler POST method processes login credentials without any rate limiting or brute-force protection. An attacker can make unlimited login attempts to brute-force credentials.

Impact: An attacker can brute-force user credentials with unlimited login attempts, eventually gaining unauthorized access to user accounts.

Fix: Implement rate limiting on the login endpoint (e.g., limit to 5 attempts per minute per IP) and add account lockout after repeated failures.
Path Injection in file upload - original file extension preserved without validation
Disabled XSS Protection header removes browser-level defense-in-depth
Debug Mode Enabled in Production - Tornado debug mode enabled displays detailed stack traces and sensitive information
Login form missing CSRF token protection
Upload form missing CSRF token protection
Server ping form missing CSRF token protection
Missing Password Field Masking - password input uses type='text' instead of type='password'
Missing Security Headers - X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy, X-Content-Type-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, and Referrer-Policy not set
Open Mode for Binary File Serving - files opened in text mode which can corrupt binary data

Weak PRNG for Uploaded Filename Generation

The random module (random.choice) is used to generate filenames for uploaded files. Python's random module uses a Mersenne Twister PRNG which is not cryptographically secure and can be predicted.

Impact: An attacker could predict generated filenames and access uploaded files. The short length (6 characters) combined with weak PRNG makes filenames guessable.

Fix: Use secrets.token_hex() or uuid.uuid4() for generating unpredictable filenames.

Outdated jQuery 1.11.1 with Known Vulnerabilities

jQuery v1.11.1 (released 2014) is included and contains multiple known CVEs including CVE-2015-9251 (XSS via cross-domain AJAX responses), CVE-2019-11358 (prototype pollution in jQuery.extend), CVE-2020-11022 and CVE-2020-11023 (XSS in jQuery.html() and jQuery DOM manipulation methods when passing untrusted HTML).

Impact: An attacker could exploit known XSS vulnerabilities in this jQuery version to execute arbitrary JavaScript in users' browsers, potentially stealing session data, PII, or performing actions on behalf of users.

Fix: Upgrade jQuery to version 3.5.0 or later which addresses all known XSS and prototype pollution vulnerabilities.
Sensitive Data Logging - HTTP request body logged to stdout without sanitization
Exposed SQLite Database File - Database stored in application root directory
Server Binding to All Network Interfaces - application binds to 0.0.0.0 exposing service on all interfaces
Unused Database Fetch Operation - spurious cur.fetchone() call after INSERT operations serves no purpose

Two Conflicting jQuery Versions Loaded

The application includes two different jQuery versions: jQuery 1.11.1 (jquery1111.min.js) and jQuery 2.1.4 (bundled in lightbox-plus-jquery.min.js). Loading multiple jQuery versions can cause unpredictable behavior and means both sets of vulnerabilities are present in the application.

Impact: Both outdated jQuery versions expose the application to their respective known vulnerabilities. The interaction between two jQuery instances may also cause unexpected behavior.

Fix: Consolidate to a single, up-to-date jQuery version (3.5.0+). Use a standalone Lightbox plugin that doesn't bundle its own jQuery.
